Luke VonDog · a living care archive

Luke VonDog.

Everything the people caring for him need — in one place.

At a glance The one thing: never stop his furosemide. For his vet, his nutritionist, and whoever is watching him.

Luke VonDog · Blue Weimaraner · neutered male · 12 yr (b. May 31, 2014) · 35.9 kg / 79.1 lb (dosing weight) · microchip 941000016858399 · allergies / drug reactions: none on record
Owner-compiled orientation to Luke's records — a summary to help his team, not a veterinary record or a directive. Currently in Bozeman, MT through September 2026.

◆

Read first — safety & goals

!

Do NOT stop the furosemide (Lasix), 300 mg/day.

Stopping risks a fatal respiratory event (per Dr. Denney). ≈ 8.4 mg/kg/day — near ACVIM Stage D.
→ If he arrives breathing hard, open-mouthed, or resting-asleep breaths > 30/min: treat as CHF decompensation.
Know this before you manage him
  • Cardiac vs renal — conditions in conflict. Diuretic-dependent (presumptive) CHF alongside IRIS Stage 2 CKD; furosemide + carprofen are both nephrotoxic.
  • The cardiac diagnosis is presumptive. No echo, no NT-proBNP, no troponin; no pimobendan / ACE-I / spironolactone — treat the dog, not the label.
  • Renal labs are ~10 months old. Creatinine 1.8 and phosphorus 5.1 are both Sep '25 — you may want fresh values before adjusting renal or cardiac meds.
  • GDV vigilance. High-risk Weimaraner; pack-mate Sam died of GDV Feb 2023; on simethicone each morning.
⚠ Please don't add another NSAID or steroid — carprofen (225 mg/day ≈ 6.3 mg/kg, owner calc) already loads kidney + liver.
Who Luke is: friendly with everyone · a brilliant, athletic beast · a lover of food and learning.

What Rikki wants for Luke — her directive, please honor it

Luke is to have a peaceful, dignified death. His health and comfort come before resuscitation.
  • DNR — no CPR.
  • No hospitalization.
  • Keep him well and comfortable; when his time comes, let him go peacefully — do not resuscitate or prolong.

1.8
↑ 1.0→1.2→1.8 · Sep '25

Sep '25 · target < 4.6

Mar '26 · lb · BCS 5/9

Values dated Sep '25 – Mar '26. Renal/liver chemistry not rechecked since Sep 2025; only Mar '26 value is BUN. A snapshot, not today's status.

❱

Who's it for — start here

For your veterinarian

What a vet wants to know

  • Never stop the furosemide (respiratory-death risk).
  • DNR · no hospitalization · comfort over resuscitation.
  • Cardiac ↔ renal conflict; both meds nephrotoxic.
  • Labs are ~10 months old — recheck before adjusting.
At a Glance System Map Data Viz Full Record
For a nutritionist

What the nutritionist wants to know

  • The cardiac-vs-renal diet tension (no shelf diet fits).
  • The omega-3 gap — he's at ~6% of the cardiac target.
  • Daily goat-cheese pill vehicle + unlimited carrots = real loads.
  • Confirm the picture first (taurine, fresh renal panel).
Nutrition Consult Diet Matrix Helping Thrive
For a pet sitter

What a pet sitter wants to know

  • His pills go in goat cheese — never skip the furosemide.
  • Food, unlimited carrots, and his Woojer mat.
  • The warning signs that mean call right now.
  • Who to call, in order.
Pet-Sitter Guide Bozeman Local-Care
Currently in Bozeman Bozeman, MT · through September 2026 — ~4,820 ft altitude increases cardiac workload; wildfire-smoke season. Local ER: Foothills Veterinary Hospital · (406) 556-0604 · fvhmt.com.
